text: The active site structure under working conditions of single-atom catalysts is unclear, which greatly hampers their design. Using iron phthalocyanine immobilized on carbon nanotubes (FePc-CNT) as a model catalyst, we explore the structure evolution of atomically dispersed iron species under working conditions for the electrochemical reduction of carbon dioxide (CO2RR) using operando 57Fe Mössbauer, X-ray absorption (XAS) and Raman spectroscopies. The iron centers are confirmed to be in the high-spin (HS) O2-Fe(III)N4 species, and they are reduced to the HS Fe(II)N4 species at the cathodic potential. Importantly, the iron centers can be reduced to metallic iron irreversibly under Ar condition, while the CO2 condition facilitates the activation of iron centers into the HS Fe(II)Pc−-CO2− species, involving a one-electron reduction of the Pc ligand, rather than that of the iron center. These experimental observations are further confirmed by theoretical calculations, showing that the electroreduction of the Pc ligand increases the d-band center of iron and thus enhances the binding of CO2, which significantly increases the activity of the iron sites. This work provides a new atomic-scale picture for understanding the cooperation between active metal centers and organic ligands in single-atom catalysts and gives an important clue for designing new efficient electrocatalysts for CO2RR.
